schools.summary: Gill ties the book to Deuteronomy as its strict continuation; Calvin reads the renewed commission as God's steadfast care, given to embolden the people more than Joshua.
Clarke defends Joshua's authorship and expounds his name as 'the salvation of Jehovah,' rendered Jesus; Wesley notes 'servant of the LORD' honours Moses yet marks him as servant, not Lord.
Henry: God honoured Moses' memory by the month's pause; Joshua, long bred to business as Moses' minister, shows that those are fittest to rule who have learnt to obey.
Keil and Delitzsch place the verse as the introductory notice of the whole book; Ellicott observes Joshua was the first man commanded to rule his conduct by a written book.

themes: Moses the servant of the LORD

schools.summary: Calvin: the notice of Moses' death confers the office on Joshua by right of succession and strengthens his authority; the promise revives confidence, for distrust makes us sluggish.
Clarke: 'servant' means God's prime minister, a title borne in like sense only by the Redeemer; Wesley: Moses the servant is dead, but God the Master lives for ever.
Keil and Delitzsch: Moses died without bringing Israel in, so Joshua must cross 'this Jordan' — the river then before him — into the land the Lord gives.
Keil and Delitzsch: the perfect 'I have given' rests on God's counsel formed long before; Ellicott: Joshua's conquest was limited — what Israel would conquer, the sole of his foot must tread.
Clarke: the words express the ease of the intended conquest — only unfaithfulness made it difficult; Wesley confines 'every place' to the bounds that follow.
Gill restricts the treading of the foot to the land of Canaan itself.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the boundaries to Deuteronomy 11:24; Ellicott reads 'the land of the Hittites' of the northern districts about Hamath and Carchemish.
Wesley and Clarke answer the objection that Israel never held the full grant: their own sloth and disobedience curtailed it, though their dominion reached the Euphrates under David and Solomon.
Calvin: the promise was not voided by delay; the people had the full bounds within their power and declined, deserving expulsion, yet divine indulgence gave them commodious habitation.
Henry: had they been obedient God would have given this and much more; the unfulfilled letter points to fulfilment in the kingdom of the Messiah.

debates.question: What is 'all the land of the Hittites'?

sides.position: the Hittites, as the most formidable tribe, stand for all the Canaanite nations
the northern districts about Hamath and Carchemish, between Palestine and the Euphrates

themes: the promise enlarged in Messiah's kingdom

schools.summary: Gill: the promise, fulfilled in Joshua, is fulfilled still more in Christ his antitype; Calvin: God points Joshua to Moses' victories, the series of favours continuing unbroken to the successor.
Henry: the same grace sufficient for those who went before shall not be wanting to those who follow; 'the Lord is with us while we are with him.'
Ellicott: Hebrews 13:5 combines this verse with Genesis 28:15 — the war-promise to Joshua becomes the church's charter against covetousness.
Clarke: the promise was most punctually and literally fulfilled; Wesley: God would withhold neither inward support nor outward assistance.

themes: I will not fail thee nor forsake thee

schools.summary: Calvin: bare promises are not sufficiently energetic without exhortation — if Joshua, remarkable for alacrity, needed spurring, how much more the sluggish; Gill sees the courage of Joshua eminent also in Christ's work of redemption.
Wesley: Joshua needed the charge because the work was great, difficult, and new, and because he had a very mean opinion of himself compared with Moses.
Keil and Delitzsch hear in 'thou shalt divide for an inheritance' the commissions of Deuteronomy 1:38 and 3:28 — firmness grounded in reliance on the promise.
Henry: it encouraged Joshua that he was sure to see the work finished; some make the frowardness of the people itself the reason to be of good courage.

themes: be strong and of a good courage

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: firmness is attained and preserved by careful observance of the law; Ellicott: the repeated charge reflects Joshua's retiring character and the peculiar courage the commanded conquest required.
Clarke: the promise was conditional on Joshua's use of every means — God will not have them who refuse to help themselves; Wesley: God's assistance was conditional and might justly be withdrawn.
Henry: no man's dignity or dominion sets him above the law of God; Joshua must govern by the law and keep it himself.
Gill: the repeated charge answers the strength of the enemy — a people more and mightier, fortified and forewarned.

Calvin: the law must not depart from the mouth because Joshua studies for the whole people, teaching others while conforming himself; intermitted meditation rusts the memory. Gill notes Jarchi restricting the book to Deuteronomy.
Ellicott: the verse is taken up in Psalms 1 — from its first appearance the written word is supreme, set above Joshua, never superseded, its authority independent of its quantity.
Clarke: Joshua must have his own copy and consult it incessantly — the obedient lack no good thing; Wesley: the law is the rule of all his private actions and public administrations.
Henry: if any man's business might have excused meditation, Joshua's might; yet he must find time, for the one thing needful may not be neglected.

themes: the supremacy of the written word

schools.summary: Gill: the commander is the great Jehovah, the everlasting I AM, faithful and able; Calvin: the emphatic question brings God's authority forward — nothing so produces confidence as assurance of his call in our own conscience.
Wesley unfolds the divine 'I': whom thou art obliged to obey, who can carry thee through, of whose faithfulness thou hast had large experience.
Henry: keep the eye on the divine warrant — 'Have not I commanded thee?' — as Christ was borne up under his sufferings by his Father's commandment.
Henry: Joshua immediately applies himself to business — he that desires office desires work; inferior magistrates are as serviceable in their places as the supreme in his.

Keil and Delitzsch: the shoterim were keepers of the family registers who levied men and circulated commands; Ellicott parallels the crossing with Sinai — three days' notice establishing the chosen leader.
Calvin: the proclamation followed the spies' return, chapter two being an interposed parenthesis; the confident 'three days' was ventured only by the suggestion of the Spirit.
Clarke distinguishes shoterim from shophetim and reports Calmet's conjecture of army heralds like Homer's, sacred persons bearing sceptres.

debates.question: Who were the shoterim, the 'officers of the people'?

sides.position: military officers or heralds proclaiming orders through the camp
keepers of the family registers, charged with levies and the circulation of commands

themes: faith fixing a date for a miracle

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch take the victuals as ordinary produce, the manna having ceased; Ellicott holds the manna continued until after the crossing, the order preparing the transition — God would not support them in idleness.

Wesley: Israel had long been allowed to buy common food and must now furnish themselves; the words were spoken after the spies returned, transpositions being frequent in scripture. Clarke: tsedah is properly prey from the conquered countries.
Henry: victuals, not transport vessels — he who bore them out of Egypt on eagles' wings would bear them into Canaan; Joshua's assurance honours the truth of God.

debates.question: Had the manna ceased when the victuals were ordered?

sides.position: yes — the provision is the natural produce of the inhabited country, the manna having already ceased
no — the manna fell until after the crossing; the order supplements it and prepares the transition to common food

schools.summary: Calvin: Joshua pleads by authority — Moses uttered only what God dictated — and by equity, since brethren must not toil while covenant partners sit at ease; Gill notes the tribes were already settled on that side.
Ellicott: the order rests on the covenant of Numbers 32, and the tribes were free to leave fighting men enough to protect their homes.

Keil and Delitzsch: Joshua quotes Moses' own recapitulation in Deuteronomy 3 rather than Numbers 32; Ellicott traces 'rest' through David and Solomon to Hebrews 4 — the last rest is Sabbatical, the others halting-places.
Henry: when God has given us rest we must ask what service we owe the unsettled, as David was restless until the ark had a habitation; beware settling upon our lees.
Clarke points to the engagements of Numbers 32:20; Wesley reads 'rest' as a place of rest and sums the appeal: his charge to you, and your promise to him.
Gill: the agreement solemnly made with Moses now falls due, and Joshua simply reminds them of it.

Clarke computes from Numbers 32:17 and Joshua 4:13 that about forty thousand crossed while seventy thousand guarded the families; Wesley: marching in front was reasonable, for their portion was secured and the van prevented desertion.
Gill: the terms were what the tribes themselves had proposed and confirmed; Calvin's rule of equity governs — some must not toil while others of the same household rest.
Henry: for these tribes the crossing was an act of self-denial and against the grain, which is why the agreement with Moses had to be produced.
Gill: 'rest, as he hath given you' means rest from their travels, such as the eastern tribes already enjoyed — the measure of their service is the mercy received.
Clarke: 'toward the sun-rising' is the east, as 'the going down of the sun' is the west — fixing the transjordanic inheritance by the sacred writer's compass.

themes: rest for all before rest for each

schools.summary: Henry: the officers of all the people answer heartily, as soldiers to their general — thus must we swear allegiance to our Lord Jesus, the captain of our salvation.

Calvin: obligations are duly discharged only when performed cheerfully; the speakers were a generation subdued by punishment, and their words extol Joshua's authority rather than their own virtue. Gill hears the deputed heads of the eastern tribes.
Ellicott: the reply belongs to the whole people, who remarkably repeat to Joshua the very words of Jehovah — 'be strong and of a good courage.'
Clarke: they acknowledge Joshua's divine mission as they had acknowledged Moses'; Wesley: the officers of all the people concur with the divine appointment.

debates.question: Who gives this answer to Joshua?

sides.position: the two tribes and a half, through their deputed heads
the officers of all the people, as representatives of the whole nation

themes: allegiance to the captain of salvation

schools.summary: Gill: the promised obedience is 'not right or wrong,' but in all things according to God's revealed will; Calvin: the form of expression is intermediate between the confidence of faith and prayer.
Wesley: 'only the LORD thy God be with thee' is not a limitation of obedience but an additional prayer; Clarke allows the conditional sense yet inclines to an earnest prayer for Joshua's prosperity.
Henry: no proviso was needed, for none suspected Joshua of deviating; the best thing we can ask for magistrates is the presence of God with them.

debates.question: Is 'only the LORD thy God be with thee' a condition of obedience or a prayer?

sides.position: a proviso — 'provided God be with thee, as with Moses, we will implicitly obey thee'
an earnest prayer for Joshua, not a limitation of their obedience

themes: the God of Moses with Joshua

schools.summary: Clarke: this was martial law, made necessary by the fathers' mutinies; and though God promised help so absolutely, he strongly insists on our co-operation — it is enough that God gives the power, our duty to use it.
Henry: most men fear the sword of the magistrate more than the justice of God; some hear an echo of the prophet like Moses, chiefly Christ, whose word refused brings a reckoning.
Gill: the rebel in view is whoever refuses to cross Jordan with his brethren at Joshua's command.
